← Back to SummariesFull text ↗

Parameter Optimization and Larger Precision for (T)FHE

IACR ePrint 2022/704 (full version; published in the Journal of Cryptology, 2023) · full view ↗

At a glance

Authors: Loris Bergerat, Anas Boudi, Quentin Bourgerie, Ilaria Chillotti, Damien Ligier, Jean-Baptiste Orfila, Samuel Tap, all at Zama (Paris). Full version on IACR ePrint 2022/704; published in the Journal of Cryptology (2023).

One sentence: The paper attacks the practical bottleneck of FHE, namely choosing cryptographic parameters that are simultaneously secure, correct, and fast, by formalizing it as a constrained optimization problem with per-operator noise and cost models, and pairs this with new large-precision techniques (a padding-free WoP-PBS and a hybrid radix/CRT integer representation) so that TFHE can compute on 16-bit and 32-bit integers efficiently.

Two intertwined contributions:

  • An optimization framework that takes a crypto-free arithmetic graph plus a target security level and failure probability, and emits an FHE graph together with its optimal parameter set. It is the first such tool for TFHE-like (bootstrapping-heavy, multi-precision) schemes.
  • Larger-precision machinery: a generalized radix/CRT/hybrid multi-ciphertext encoding of large integers, two new homomorphic modular-reduction algorithms for arbitrary modulus, and a new WoP-PBS (programmable bootstrap without a bit of padding) that evaluates a generic LUT over a multi-ciphertext integer.

Headline findings:

  • The classical TFHE PBS only scales to about 5 bits of message precision before becoming impractical (each extra bit roughly doubles the cost above 4 to 5 bits), and no feasible parameters exist beyond $\approx 11$ bits.
  • For larger precision it is far better to split a message across several small ciphertexts than to inflate parameters for one. The new WoP-PBS scales roughly linearly (not exponentially) in message size for generic LUTs.
  • Concrete benchmarks (TFHE-rs, single Xeon, 128-bit security): a generic 16-bit LUT in $\approx 0.76$ to $0.82\,\text{s}$; 16-bit homomorphic multiply in $\approx 0.4\,\text{s}$ (CRT) to $\approx 5.7\,\text{s}$ (radix); 32-bit multiply from $\approx 1.8\,\text{s}$ (hybrid) up.
  • Security is enforced via the lattice-estimator as a security oracle; correctness via Gaussian-tail failure-probability bounds; the whole search reduces to optimizing a single atomic pattern, solvable in well under a second.
  • All parameters are produced by the framework and all benchmarks run on TFHE-rs, Zama's successor to the Concrete library, so the techniques directly underpin Zama's integer/Concrete stack.

The problem: secure AND correct AND fast, all at once

In theory FHE computes anything over ciphertexts; in practice the obstacle is parameter selection. Every TFHE-like computation must fix many knobs, which the authors split into macro-parameters (LWE dimension $n$, GLWE dimension $k$, polynomial size $N$, ciphertext modulus $q$, noise levels) and micro-parameters (per-operator decomposition base $\beta$ and number of levels $\ell$ for a key-switch or a bootstrap). These must jointly satisfy three guarantees:

  • Security: the (R)LWE instance must be hard. The product $k\cdot N$ (or $n$ for LWE), the secret-key distribution, the noise, and $q$ determine the level; the LWE/lattice-estimator [APS15] judges it. Security is easy in the sense that one can always add encryption noise to reach the target, so the search reduces to picking dimensions and then taking the minimal secure noise.
  • Correctness: noise grows through each operator; if it crosses a threshold, decryption returns the wrong message. One must track noise via per-operator noise formulae and keep it below a bound at every edge of the circuit.
  • Cost: among all correct, secure choices, minimize a cost model (here, single-thread running time approximated by operation counts).

The central question of Section 3 is: for a given graph of FHE operators, how to find parameters so the evaluation is the fastest while preserving both correctness and security? The authors note this was an open problem before their contribution, and that existing FHE compilers either target batched, bootstrap-free schemes (BGV/BFV/CKKS, where the lever is multiplicative depth) or use hard-coded parameters for Boolean TFHE circuits.

Encoding, ciphertexts, and the failure criterion

The plaintext encoding fixes a ciphertext modulus $q$, a message modulus $p$, and $\pi$ padding bits with $2^{\pi}\cdot p \le q$. A message $M$ is encoded as $\widetilde{M} = \lfloor \Delta\cdot M \rceil$ with the scaling factor $\Delta = \frac{q}{2^{\pi}\cdot p}$, and decoded by $\lfloor \widetilde{M}/\Delta \rceil$. In practice the encoding carries a small error polynomial $E=\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} e_i X^i$, so $\widetilde{M}=\Delta\cdot M + E$, and decoding fails as soon as some coefficient satisfies $|e_i|\ge \Delta/2$:

$$P\!\left(\bigcup_i |e_i|\ge \tfrac{\Delta}{2}\right) = P\!\left(\mathrm{Decode}(\widetilde{M},\,2^{\pi}\cdot p,\,q)\neq M\right)$$

Equation 1 is the quantity the whole correctness analysis bounds. A GLWE ciphertext of $\widetilde{M}$ under secret key $\vec{S}=(S_1,\dots,S_k)\in\mathcal{R}_q^k$ is the tuple $(A_1,\dots,A_k,\,B=\sum_{i=1}^{k}A_i\cdot S_i+\widetilde{M}+E)$ with the $A_i$ uniform in $\mathcal{R}_q=\mathbb{Z}_q[X]/(X^N+1)$. Setting $N=1$ gives an LWE ciphertext (then $n=k$); setting $k=1,N\gt 1$ gives an RLWE ciphertext. A GGSW ciphertext is a redundant stack of $(k+1)\ell$ GLWE ciphertexts parameterized by a decomposition base $\beta$ and a number of levels $\ell$; GGSW ciphertexts are used for bootstrapping keys and circuit bootstrapping.

The three TFHE workhorses are: the key switch (KS), which changes the secret key at the price of added noise; the programmable bootstrap (PBS), a modulus switch (MS) then blind rotation (BR) then sample extraction (SE) that simultaneously reduces noise and applies a LUT $L$; and the homomorphic dot product (DP). When a KS feeds a PBS the pair is written KS-PBS. The generalized PBS of [CLOT21] adds two parameters $(\kappa,\vartheta)$ controlling exactly which part of the plaintext the modulus switch extracts.

Failure probability: from Gaussian tails to a noise bound

Correctness is made quantitative through the standard score

$$z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}}) = \sqrt{2}\cdot \mathrm{erf}^{-1}\!\left(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}\right)$$

Equation 2 says a centered Gaussian of standard deviation $\sigma$ lands outside $(-z^{*}\sigma,\,z^{*}\sigma)$ with probability at most $p_{\mathrm{fail}}$. Since decoding fails exactly when a coefficient exceeds $\Delta/2$, the authors derive an explicit noise bound:

$$t_{\alpha}(\pi,p) = \frac{\Delta}{2\cdot z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}})}, \qquad p_{\mathrm{fail}} = 1-\sqrt[N]{1-\alpha}$$

Equation 3 guarantees correct decoding with probability $\ge 1-\alpha$ whenever $\sigma \le t_{\alpha}$. The $N$-th root comes from a union bound over the $N$ coefficients (for an LWE ciphertext $N=1$, so $p_{\mathrm{fail}}=\alpha$). The tightness of the noise model is what makes the confidence interval, and hence the chosen parameters, trustworthy.

This turns correctness into a clean set membership. With $\{\sigma_i\}$ the noise standard deviations on every edge, the noise feasible set is the intersection of per-edge constraints

$$\mathcal{S} = \bigcap_{i\in I}\mathcal{S}_i = \left\{\, x\in\mathcal{P} \;\middle|\; \forall i\in[\![1,|L|]\!],\ \sigma_i(x)\le t_{\alpha}(p_i) \,\right\}$$

Equation 4 collects all parameter choices that yield a correct computation, drawn from the search space $\mathcal{P}$ (the Cartesian product of every micro- and macro-parameter search space, e.g. $\mathcal{P}_N=\{2^8,\dots,2^{17}\}$, $\mathcal{P}_n=[\![256,2048]\!]$, $\mathcal{P}_k=[\![1,6]\!]$). The FHE optimization problem is then

$$\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{P}}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\mathcal{G},x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$$

Equation 5 is a constrained minimization that classical solvers (branch-and-bound) can attack. The remaining difficulty is that $\mathcal{S}$ depends on the whole graph, which can be huge.

The noise and cost model, and the security oracle

Each FHE operator is equipped with three things: a plain operator (the cleartext function it must reproduce), a noise model (a formula giving the output noise variance from the input variances, the cryptographic parameters, and the plaintext values), and a cost model (a surrogate for the metric to minimize). Noise is always a variance of a centered Gaussian; more noise means larger variance.

The security oracle is built from the lattice-estimator: given $k\cdot N$, a security level $\lambda$ and modulus $q$, it returns the minimal noise variance $\sigma_{\min}^2$ needed for security. This decouples security from the optimization: once dimensions are chosen, the encryption noise is pinned to the oracle's value, so the optimizer never wastes effort searching noise.

The cost model counts the algorithmic work of each operator (additions, multiplications, integer casts, and the asymptotic FFT cost) as a proxy for single-thread time. For instance a polynomial dot product $\sum_{i=1}^{\alpha} M_i\cdot CT_i$ (with $M_i$ polynomials and $CT_i=(A_i,B_i)$ RLWE ciphertexts) costs

$$\underbrace{(2+1)\,\alpha\, N\log N}_{\text{to FFT domain}} + \underbrace{2\alpha N}_{\text{float }\times} + \underbrace{(\alpha-1)N}_{\text{float }+} + \underbrace{2N\log N}_{\text{to standard domain}}$$

Equation 6 treats a float multiply as costing the same as an integer add, which is close enough in practice to yield efficient parameters. A subtle but important detail: because the TFHE PBS uses an FFT in $f64$, the authors add an empirical FFT-noise correction to the bootstrap output variance,

$$n\cdot 2^{\omega_1}\cdot \ell\cdot \beta^2\cdot N^2\cdot (k+1), \qquad \omega_1 \approx 22 - 2.6$$

Equation 7 captures mantissa-bit loss (the $22 = 2\cdot(64-53)$ term comes from the 53-bit $f64$ mantissa against $q=2^{64}$) plus an experimental fit of $2.6$.

Shrinking the search: atomic patterns, domination, and Pareto fronts

The key simplification is to chop the graph at the operators whose output noise is independent of their input noise. The authors split operators into category (i), which output a noise independent of the input (the PBS), and category (ii), which add to the input noise (the KS or the dot product). Between two consecutive PBSs lies an atomic pattern (AP): a sub-graph whose noise can be analyzed in isolation. The canonical AP type $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ is a dot product, then a key switch, then a PBS, and it is fully characterized by two numbers: the dot-product 2-norm $\nu$ and the noise bound $t$, written $A=A(\nu,t)$. The 2-norm controls how the DP scales input noise: for weights $\{\omega_i\}$ the output variance is $\nu_2^2\sigma^2$ with

$$\nu_2^2 = \sum_{i\in I}\omega_i^2$$

Equation 8 is the squared 2-norm of the integer weights. This admits a domination relation (Theorem 1): if $\nu_1\le\nu_2$ and $t_2\le t_1$ then $\mathcal{S}(A_2(\nu_2,t_2))\subset\mathcal{S}(A_1(\nu_1,t_1))$, so the harder AP dominates the easier one; satisfying its constraint satisfies the other's. Therefore one only needs the non-dominated APs, that is the Pareto front $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}}=\mathrm{Pareto}(\mathcal{G})$, and $\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})=\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}})$. Because the dot-product cost is taken negligible, the cost of an AP of this type is independent of the particular instance, so minimizing the whole-graph cost reduces to minimizing one worst-case atomic pattern:

$$\arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{P}}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\cdot,x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{pareto}})$$

Equation 9 is the simplified problem actually solved. Two bracketing graphs, $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{worst}}=\{A(\max_i\nu_i,\ \min_i t_i)\}$ and $\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{best}}$, give an upper and a lower bound on the optimum, and coincide (optimal) when the graph is uniform. A further shortcut: in $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ the noise increases monotonically until the modulus switch inside the final PBS, so the bound need only be checked at that one point. This machinery also lets one fairly compare different bootstrapping algorithms: a noisier bootstrap simply shows up as a higher input noise for the next AP.

Why large precision needs many ciphertexts: TFHE PBS vs Tree-PBS

Using the framework to price operators yields the paper's central practical message. The plain TFHE PBS ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$) is the best tool for 1 to 5 bit messages, but its cost is essentially exponential: above 4 to 5 bits each extra bit of precision roughly doubles the cost (the polynomial size $N$ must double), and beyond $\approx 11$ bits no correct parameters exist at all. For $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ the noise bound is $t(p,1)=\frac{q}{2^{1+1}\cdot p\cdot z^{*}(p_{\mathrm{fail}})}$.

The alternative for larger integers is to split the message across blocks and use the Tree-PBS of Guimaraes, Borin and Aranha ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$), whose first layer uses a multi-value PBS [CIM19]. Because a Tree-PBS with $i$ blocks involves several PBSs, its noise bound is computed from the per-AP PBS count

$$\eta_i = i\cdot\frac{p^{\,i-1}-1}{p-1} + 1$$

Equation 10 gives the number of PBSs over which the global failure probability must be shared (each PBS is allotted $p_i' = 1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}})^{1/\eta_i}$). Tree-PBS reaches higher precision (up to 16 bits with 2 blocks, 21 bits with 3 blocks) but its cost explodes with the number of blocks: a generic 21-bit LUT via Tree-PBS costs more than $2^{20}\times$ a Boolean PBS, and it only becomes the cheapest of the three from about 10 bits. The takeaway: above $\approx 9$ bits even Tree-PBS is too expensive, motivating a better generic-LUT algorithm.

Figure 1: cost (log) versus message precision for the single-ciphertext TFHE PBS ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$, blue dots) against the Tree-PBS with 2 and 3 blocks ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$, red plus and green triangles), at four dot-product 2-norms $\log_2(\nu)\in\{4,6,8,10\}$, with $q=2^{64}$, $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\approx 2^{-35}$ and one padding bit. The single-ciphertext curve stops near 11 bits; splitting into blocks reaches further but grows fast.

Encoding large integers: carry buffers, radix, CRT, and the hybrid

To compute on large integers, a message is decomposed into several small LWE ciphertexts. The authors first refine the single-ciphertext layout: the plaintext space is split into a message subspace (an integer mod $\beta$), a carry subspace (room to absorb carries from leveled additions/multiplications), and a bit of padding for bootstrapping. A metadata called the degree of fullness, $\deg(\mathrm{ct})=\frac{\mu}{p-1}\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $\mu$ the worst-case message value, tracks how full the carry buffer is; while it stays below 1, additions and scalar multiplications proceed without a bootstrap, and a PBS is only triggered to flush carries and reduce noise. The authors also handle a PBS with $p$ not a power of two by carefully sizing the redundant mega-cases of the LUT polynomial:

$$\widetilde{L} = X^{-\lfloor \frac{N}{2p}\rceil}\cdot\left(\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\left\lfloor \frac{q}{p'}\cdot y_{\lfloor i\cdot p/N\rfloor}\right\rceil\cdot X^i\right)$$

Equation 11 is the redundant LUT polynomial; with $p$ not a power of two the mega-cases take size $\lfloor N/p\rfloor$ or $\lceil N/p\rceil$, and the failure analysis uses the worst case $\lfloor N/p\rfloor$.

Two classical multi-ciphertext schemes are then generalized:

  • Radix: a number mod $\Omega=\prod_i\beta_i$ stored as digits in mixed bases, like ordinary base-10 notation, with $\mathrm{msg}=m_0+\sum_{i=1}^{\kappa-1}m_i\cdot\prod_{j=0}^{i-1}\beta_j$. Cheap schoolbook carry propagation, but historically limited to $\Omega$ that factor into small bases (no arbitrary, e.g. prime, modulus). Good practice keeps $p_i\le 2^8$.
  • CRT: residues $x_i = x \bmod \omega_i$ for co-prime $\omega_i$, each in its own ciphertext, giving independent parallel arithmetic, but limited by the scarcity of small ($\lt 8$-bit) co-primes. A native CRT variant drops the padding bit and carry buffer (encoding $m_i$ as $\lceil \frac{q}{\beta_i}\cdot m_i\rceil$), making additions and scalar multiplications essentially free LWE operations, at the price of needing a WoP-PBS for any LUT.

The new hybrid representation (Figure 2) puts a CRT layer on top and a radix layer beneath each residue, recovering both the CRT's parallelism and the radix's ability to represent any modulus. It strictly generalizes pure-CRT (all $\kappa_i=1$) and pure-radix ($\kappa=1$). The paper also contributes two new algorithms for homomorphic modular reduction for any modulus in the radix approach, closing a previously open gap.

Figure 2: the hybrid encoding. A message $\mathrm{msg}\bmod\Omega$ is first split by CRT into residues $\mathrm{msg}_i=\mathrm{msg}\bmod\Omega_i$, and each residue is then represented in radix with its own bases $(\beta_{i,\kappa_i-1},\dots,\beta_{i,0})$.

Two new modular-reduction algorithms for radix

Generalizing radix to any $\Omega$ with $\prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_j \lt \Omega \lt \prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-1}\beta_j$ only requires a homomorphic modular reduction. The authors give two.

The first method reduces the most significant block (which carries $m_{\kappa-1}\cdot\prod_{i=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_i$ and may exceed $\Omega$) by evaluating $\kappa$ LUTs on it, each extracting one digit of $\big(x\cdot\prod_{h=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_h\bmod\Omega\big)$ in the base decomposition, then adding the lower digits back into the lower blocks:

Algorithm 1: ModReduction1((ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}), PUB)
  Context: P_j is the r-redundant LUT for
      Z_{p_{k-1}} -> Z_{beta_j},  x |-> x'_j = Decomp_j( x * prod_{h=0}^{k-2} beta_h  mod Omega )
  Input:   (ct_0, ..., ct_{k-1}) encrypting msg = m_0 + sum_{i=1}^{k-1} m_i * prod_{j=0}^{i-1} beta_j
           PUB: public material for KS-PBS
  Output:  (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1}) encrypting msg mod Omega

  // Decompose block k-1 with respect to base (beta_0, ..., beta_{k-1})
  1  for j in [0 .. k-1]:
  2      c_j <- KS-PBS(ct_{k-1}, PUB, P_j)
  // Add decomposition into all blocks up to k-2
  3  for j in [0 .. k-2]:
  4      ct'_j <- Add(ct_j, c_j)
  // Replace block k-1 by the (k-1)-th element of the decomposition
  5  ct'_{k-1} <- c_{k-1}
  6  return (ct'_0, ..., ct'_{k-1})

The $\kappa$ KS-PBSs in line 2 can be batched with PBSmanyLUT [CLOT21] or multi-value bootstrapping [CIM19].

The second method exploits the radix decomposition of the negated scaling factor $-\prod_{h=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_h\bmod\Omega=\nu_0+\nu_1\beta_0+\dots+\nu_{\kappa-1}\prod_{j=0}^{\kappa-2}\beta_j$. If $\nu_{\kappa-1}=0$ and the other $\nu_j$ are small (ideally many zero), it just multiplies the MSB block by the constants $\nu_j$ and subtracts from the lower blocks. For a 3-block integer with $\beta_0\beta_1=\nu_0+\nu_1\beta_0+\nu_2\beta_0\beta_1\bmod\Omega$,

Algorithm 2 (worked example): m = m_0 + m_1*beta_0 + m_2*beta_0*beta_1
  m = (m_0 + m_2*nu_0) + (m_1 + m_2*nu_1)*beta_0 + (m_2*nu_2)*beta_0*beta_1   mod Omega
  // if nu_2 = 0 the last block is emptied
  Example: Omega = 1055, k = 3, beta = 25, p = 27;  25^2 mod 1055 = -31 = -1*25 + 1
           so nu_0 = 1, nu_1 = -1, nu_2 = 0:
    block 0  <- block 0 + 1 * block 2
    block 1  <- block 1 + (-1) * block 2
    block 2  <- encryption of 0

This second method needs only constant multiplications and subtractions (plus a carry propagation if a buffer fills), so it is cheaper than the first whenever the $\nu_j$ are small.

The new WoP-PBS: a padding-free generic LUT over many ciphertexts

The headline algorithmic contribution (Section 4.2, Algorithm 3) is a WoP-PBS, a programmable bootstrap that needs no bit of padding, accepts one or several input LWE ciphertexts, can round/truncate each input to a chosen precision, and can evaluate several LUTs on the same inputs for roughly the price of one. It is built from two TFHE primitives, circuit bootstrapping and (mixed/vertical/horizontal) packing [CGGI20], in three stages:

Algorithm 3 (structure): WoP-PBS over kappa input blocks
  Input:  (ct_0, ..., ct_{kappa-1}); LUTs L; public keys (BSK, KSK, ...)
  // Stage 1 -- Bit Extract (lines 2..12): peel every message bit into its own LWE
  for i in [0 .. kappa-1]:
      shift ct_i so the next bit reaches the MSB (multiply by 2^{alpha_i - 1}, alpha_i = ceil(log2(beta_i)))
      add correction term epsilon_i                     // avoids sign errors near 0 and q/2
      LWE_{i,bit} <- KS-PBS(ct_i, scaled negacyclic sign function)   // generalized PBS reads next bit
      subtract LWE_{i,bit} from ct_i                     // clears the extracted bit; chain LSB -> MSB
  // Stage 2 -- Circuit Bootstrap (CBS): turn each bit-LWE into a GGSW ciphertext
  for each extracted bit:
      GGSW_bit <- CircuitBootstrap(LWE_bit)              // dominant cost; linear in #bits
  // Stage 3 -- Mixed/Vertical packing (VPLut, line 13): GGSW bits drive a CMux tree
  out <- VPLut(GGSW bits, L)                             // CMux tree + blind rotation + sample extract
  return out                                             // ( LWE(l_0(m)), ..., LWE(l_{kappa-1}(m)) )

The bit-extraction correction term, derived to avoid a wrong sign when an encoded value sits near 0 or $q/2$ (including for non-power-of-two bases $\beta_i=\beta_i'\cdot 2^k$), is

$$\epsilon_i = \left\lfloor \frac{q\cdot 2^{k-2}}{\beta_i}\right\rfloor$$

Equation 12 (the small $2^{\alpha_i-3}$ term being negligible). Circuit bootstrapping is the dominant cost, so the algorithm scales linearly in the number of message bits until, for very large inputs (about 28 bits with their parameters), the packing stage overtakes it. The paper gives closed-form variance formulae for the circuit bootstrap ($\mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{CB}})$, summing PBS and private functional KS terms) and for the full WoP-PBS, $\mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{WoP\text{-}PBS}}) = \mathrm{Var}(E_{\mathrm{CB}}) + (\sum_{i=0}^{\kappa-1}\delta_i)\cdot(\text{mixed-packing terms})$, derived from the [CLOT21] noise models.

Several optimizations are noted: chaining the bit-extraction PBSs from LSB to MSB; replacing the packing key-switches with an initial LWE-to-GLWE KS plus an external product (after [CCR19]) to shrink keys; using a generalized PBS so the modulus switch directly reads the next bit; and using PBSmanyLUT to do all PBSs of a circuit bootstrap at once. For special LUTs (ReLU, sign in radix mode) only the MSBs matter, so packing collapses and the cost becomes linear in the number of blocks. Public material is also far smaller than Tree-PBS: for 18 bits of precision a Tree-PBS key is 1.65 GB versus 0.926 GB for the new WoP-PBS.

Figure 3: cleartext view of the new WoP-PBS on a toy 16-bit message split into blocks $m_0,\dots,m_3$. Each block is bit-extracted and circuit-bootstrapped into GGSW bits, which then drive a CMux tree (vertical packing) plus a final select-in-LUT (blind rotation) to produce $L[m]$.

Comparing the operators: where each bootstrap wins

With all three operators priced by the same framework (and held to the same $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\approx 2^{-35}$ and 2-norm), the comparison becomes apples-to-apples. The new WoP-PBS ($\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$, in 1/2/4-block variants) shares its failure budget over the $\kappa$ input ciphertexts via $p_i' = 1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}})^{1/\kappa}$ and uses the noise bound $t(p,0)=\frac{q}{2^{1}\cdot p\cdot z^{*}(p_i')}$ (note: no padding bit). It scales far better than Tree-PBS for generic LUTs on large precision and, unlike the classical PBS, needs no padding bit. Concretely it finds solutions up to 24 bits; at 18 bits the 2-block variant is $\approx 2^{7}\times$ faster than the 3-block Tree-PBS, and a 24-bit LUT costs only $\approx 2^{10}\times$ a 1-bit classical PBS.

The same framework also compares the new WoP-PBS against the competing padding-free bootstrap of [LMP21], which (using two [CGGI20] PBSs as subroutines) finds no feasible solution above $\approx 10$ bits. The practical division of labour that emerges: classical PBS for 1 to 5 bits, and the WoP-PBS (over multi-ciphertext integers) for everything larger, with Tree-PBS rarely the best choice.

Figure 4: cost (log) versus precision comparing $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ (single-ciphertext PBS, blue dots), $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{GBA21})}$ (Tree-PBS, 2/3 blocks), and the new $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ WoP-PBS in 1/2/4-block variants (magenta), at four 2-norms. The WoP-PBS curves rise much more gently with precision than Tree-PBS.

Concrete parameters and benchmarks (TFHE-rs / Concrete)

All benchmarks run on TFHE-rs, the Rust successor to Zama's Concrete library [CJL+20], on a single Xeon Platinum 8375C, with all parameters produced by the optimization framework, 128-bit security, and (by default) $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\le 2^{-13.9}$ (standard score 4). Each parameter set reports, per atomic pattern, the LWE dimension $n$ and noise, the GLWE pair $(k,\log_2 N)$ and noise, and the decomposition base/level for the key-switch, PBS, packing KS and circuit bootstrap. For example $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ set #1 ($p=2^2$, $\nu=3$) uses $n=615,\,k=4,\,N=2^9$; set #6 ($p=2^8$, $\nu=17$) uses $n=983,\,k=1,\,N=2^{14}$. WoP-PBS sets #8 to #12 specify how many bits to extract per block and the number $\kappa$ of input ciphertexts; sets #11/#12 encode the native-CRT bases. The biggest 2-norm a block can ever face is $\lfloor\frac{p-1}{\beta-1}\rfloor$. The framework can also generate compatible pairs of parameter sets (matching $k\cdot N$ and absorbing each other's output noise) so a circuit switches freely between classical-PBS arithmetic and WoP-PBS LUTs; this works for couples (#1,#8) and (#2,#9) by optimizing $\mathcal{A}^{(\text{this work})}$ first, but (#3,#10) needs the order reversed.

Representative timings:

  • 16-bit radix ($\Omega=2^{16}$): addition $\approx 6.7$ to $12.8\,\mu\text{s}$, multiplication $\approx 5.7$ to $29\,\text{s}$, carry cleaning $\approx 0.66$ to $0.93\,\text{s}$, generic LUT via WoP-PBS $\approx 0.82$ to $1.80\,\text{s}$.
  • 16-bit CRT ($\Omega=2^3\cdot 3^2\cdot 7\cdot 11\cdot 13\approx 2^{16}$): multiply $\approx 0.40\,\text{s}$ sequential ($\approx 80\,\text{ms}$ on 5 threads); a polynomial-function LUT is as cheap as a per-residue carry cleaning. A generic non-CRT-friendly LUT is slower ($\approx 23\,\text{s}$) when parameters were tuned for $\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$ compatibility.
  • 16-bit native CRT ($\Omega=7\cdot 8\cdot 9\cdot 11\cdot 13$): multiply $\approx 7.4\,\text{s}$, LUT $\approx 3.8\,\text{s}$ sequential ($\approx 0.76\,\text{s}$ on 5 threads), additions free ($\approx 4\,\mu\text{s}$).
  • 32-bit: radix multiply $\approx 9.6$ to $43.8\,\text{s}$ depending on block count; hybrid (CRT over $\Omega=2^5\cdot 3^5\cdot 5^4\cdot 7^4$) multiply $\approx 1.8\,\text{s}$ on 4 threads; native CRT ($\Omega=3\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 19\cdot 23\cdot 29\cdot 31\cdot 32$) multiply $\approx 5.3\,\text{s}$ on 8 threads (or $36.8\,\text{s}$ sequential). Generic LUT on 32-bit integers is impractical. A caution (Remark 12): with 32 single-bit blocks the number of APs in a multiply is quadratic in block count, inflating the overall failure probability, another reason fewer, larger blocks (hybrid) win at 32 bits.

The parameter search itself is fast: most curves are generated in $\lt 1\,\text{s}$; only Tree-PBS (no shortcuts implemented) takes $\approx 26\,\text{min}$.

The full framework: whole-graph failure, PBS insertion, key positions

Section 5 lifts the per-AP analysis to a full-fledged framework: the input is a crypto-free plain DAG (additions, multiplications, LUTs, each edge labelled by precision and public/private), and the output is simultaneously the best FHE DAG and its parameter set, chosen over a space of translation rules (e.g. a multiply becomes two PBSs [CJL+20] or a leveled multiply [CLOT21]):

$$(\widehat{\mathcal{G}},\widehat{x}) = \arg\min_{\mathcal{G},x}\ \mathrm{Cost}(\mathcal{G},x) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \mathcal{G}\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{FHE}}(\mathcal{G}),\ \ x\in\mathcal{S}(\mathcal{G})$$

Equation 13 is the joint graph-and-parameter optimization. Several features sharpen the result:

  • Whole-graph failure probability: individual-AP failure probabilities combine as an upper bound

$$p_{\mathrm{fail}}(\mathcal{G}) \le 1-\prod_{i\in I}\bigl(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_i)\bigr)$$

Equation 14, and using domination the dominant AP is allotted $p_{\mathrm{fail}}(A_{\mathrm{dom}})=1-(1-p_{\mathrm{fail}}(\mathcal{G}))^{1/|I'|}$ and the dominated ones get a tighter score $\kappa_1$,

$$\kappa_1 = \frac{\Delta_1}{2}\cdot\left(D\cdot(\nu_1^2-\nu_2^2) + \Bigl(\frac{\Delta_2}{2\kappa}\Bigr)^2\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$$

Equation 15 (with $D=\min_x\sigma_{\mathrm{BR}}^2(x)$), so the user specifies a single target failure probability for the whole use-case rather than per operator, iteratively rebalancing until within tolerance.

  • Optimal PBS insertion (Theorem 2): when a dot product has a large 2-norm it forces large, slow parameters; the framework can automatically split the DP into sub-DPs joined by extra PBSs, trading more bootstraps for smaller, cheaper parameters. The optimal two-way split solves

$$\min_{\omega_{i,1},\Lambda}\ \max(\nu_1,\nu_2) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \omega_i=\omega_{i,1}\Lambda+\omega_{i,2},\ \ \gcd(\omega_{i,1},\Lambda)=1,\ \ \nu_j=\sqrt{\textstyle\sum_i\omega_{j,1}^2}$$

Equation 16, which balances the two halves' 2-norms.

  • Key-switch position (Theorem 3): it is analytically always at least as good to place the KS immediately before the PBS (the [CJP21] ordering) as elsewhere (e.g. the [CGGI20] gate-bootstrapping ordering, where the KS noise gets amplified by the DP). The framework formalizes and exploits this, and the KS-free variant is always worst.
  • Multiple public keys (Theorems 4 to 5): allowing several bootstrap/key-switch keys lets high-noise-budget parts of a graph use smaller, faster keys; a monotonicity result (the optimal key assignment $\vec{\delta}$ satisfies $\delta_i\ge\delta_{i+1}$ along increasing 2-norm) prunes the assignment search.
  • Failure-probability spectrum: the cost of tightening $p_{\mathrm{fail}}$ from $2^{-14}$ down to $2^{-50}$ is quantified (Figure 5). For the single-ciphertext PBS, tightening can jump the cost when $N$ must quadruple rather than double; for the WoP-PBS the curves merely shift up.
  • Consensus-friendly TFHE: by avoiding the FFT (which is non-deterministic across implementations), an atomic pattern can be made bit-reproducible, useful for blockchain settings where all nodes must agree on the exact ciphertext.

Figure 5: cost (log) versus precision for the single-ciphertext PBS ($\mathcal{A}^{(\mathrm{CJP21})}$) at four target failure probabilities $p_{\mathrm{fail}}\in\{2^{-14},2^{-20},2^{-35},2^{-50}\}$ and fixed $\log_2(\nu)=4$. Each extra bit roughly doubles the cost, and tighter failure targets shift the curve upward (with jumps where $N$ must grow $4\times$).

Relation to TFHE and takeaways

This work sits squarely on top of the TFHE line [CGGI20] and its programmable bootstrap, and on the generalized PBS / first WoP-PBS of [CLOT21]. Its contribution is twofold and complementary: a methodology (the optimization framework) that finally makes choosing secure, correct and fast parameters an automatable problem for bootstrapping-heavy schemes, and a set of large-precision constructions (hybrid radix/CRT integers, arbitrary-modulus modular reduction, and the padding-free multi-ciphertext WoP-PBS) that make 16-bit and 32-bit homomorphic integer arithmetic and generic LUTs practical.

Practically, the results are realized in TFHE-rs, the successor to Zama's Concrete library, and the framework is the engine behind Concrete's automatic parameter selection, so the paper is effectively the design document for how Zama's compiler turns a user's plain program into a secure, correct, fast FHE circuit. Recurring lessons: for large precision, prefer several small ciphertexts over one big one; the classical TFHE PBS is the right tool only up to $\approx 5$ bits; and a faithful, FFT-corrected noise model plus the lattice-estimator are what let the optimizer trust its own answers. Stated future work includes optimizing the integer representation itself (moduli, block counts), optimizing the graph topology, and extending the framework to non-TFHE schemes.
